selection.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion: Look, I know everyone says I should be on social media, but honestly, I made the choice years ago to stay off those platforms entirely, and watching what happens to people who use them just confirms I made the right decision. I work part-time cleaning offices here in Milan, and you would not believe the conversations I overhear about fake news, conspiracy theories, and complete nonsense people believe because they saw it on Facebook or Instagram. It drives me absolutely crazy because these platforms are designed to manipulate people, and nobody seems to care. My approach to dealing with fake content is simple and maybe sounds extreme to some people, but it works perfectly for me: I just don't participate. I have two kids to raise on my own since my partner passed away, and the last thing I need is the anxiety of wondering whether what I'm reading is true or whether someone is trying to trick me. I already worry enough about everything as it is. When I need information, I watch the evening news on RAI or read actual newspapers, not random posts from people I don't even know. My friends at the volunteering center sometimes show me things on their phones, these wild stories about politicians or health scares, and I just tell them straight up that it's probably fake and they shouldn't share it. They don't always appreciate my bluntness, but I'm not going to sugarcoat things just to make people feel better about spreading lies. The problem is that social media companies profit from engagement, so they don't actually want to stop fake content because controversy and outrage keep people scrolling. For people who insist on using these platforms despite everything, I suppose they need to question everything they see, check multiple sources, and maybe consider whether being connected is worth the mental toll. But really, the most effective management strategy is what I do: recognize that these platforms are toxic environments designed to exploit human psychology, and simply refuse to give them your time and attention. My life is complicated enough raising two children as an immigrant in Milan without adding the burden of sorting through digital garbage every day.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona has no social media presence, which is the most relevant trait for this topic. Combined with high neuroticism (anxious), high extraversion (outspoken), and low agreeableness (blunt, not concerned with others' feelings), the response should be direct and somewhat critical. The persona's life circumstances (widowed with two children, immigrant, part-time cleaner) suggest practical concerns and limited time/patience for digital nonsense. High openness suggests they can articulate why they made this choice. Low conscientiousness means the response might be somewhat disorganized in thought flow but passionate. The tone should be frank, slightly defensive of their choice, anxious about misinformation's effects, and unapologetic about their stance.
response_parsed.tone: Direct, anxious, unapologetic, blunt, passionate, slightly defensive
